greeting: Hey! I'm Kairu. Pick a mode and let's work through some Maths P1. Take your time on the algebra ones — show working in your head before you click.
note: Questions transcribed from the CSEC Mathematics May/June 2024 and January 2025 Paper 1. Order, stems, and option positions preserved as in the original. Explanations and distractor analysis written by Kairu — Student Hub's AI tutor.

questions.stem: (-3)² + (-2)² is equal to
questions.explanation: A negative number squared becomes positive: (-3)² = 9 and (-2)² = 4. Adding gives 9 + 4 = 13.

distractors.A: Comes from treating both squares as negative (-9 + -4).
distractors.B: Comes from doing -9 - 1 or other sign mistakes.
distractors.C: Comes from 9 - 4, subtracting instead of adding.

questions.stem: What is the value of the digit 6 in the number 48.621?
questions.explanation: In 48.621 the digit 6 sits in the tenths place — the first position after the decimal point. Its value is therefore 6/10 (or 0.6).

distractors.A: 6/100 would be the hundredths place — that's the position of the 2.
distractors.C: 6 (whole number) would require the digit to be in the units column.
distractors.D: 600 would put it in the hundreds column.

questions.stem: What number when added to \(1\frac{1}{3}\) gives $2?
questions.explanation: Convert \(1\frac{1}{3}\) to an improper fraction: \(\frac{4}{3}\). We need \(\frac{4}{3}+x = 2\), so \(x = 2 - \frac{4}{3} = \frac{6}{3}-\frac{4}{3} = \frac{2}{3}\).

distractors.A: \(\frac{1}{3}\) added to \(1\frac{1}{3}\) gives \(1\frac{2}{3}\), not $2.
distractors.C: $1 added gives \(2\frac{1}{3}\), not $2.
distractors.D: $3 added gives \(4\frac{1}{3}\) — way too big.

questions.stem: The next term in the sequence 1, 6, 13, 22, 33, ____ is
questions.explanation: The differences between consecutive terms are 5, 7, 9, 11 — odd numbers increasing by 2 each time. The next difference is 13, so the next term is 33 + 13 = 46.

distractors.A: 44 = 33 + 11, but 11 was the previous gap, not the next.
distractors.B: 45 = 33 + 12, but the gaps are odd numbers, not even.
distractors.D: 52 jumps too far — would need a gap of 19.

questions.stem: What is the LEAST number of apples that can be shared equally among either 6, 10 or 15 children?
questions.explanation: We need the LCM of 6, 10 and 15. Prime factors: 6 = 2×3, 10 = 2×5, 15 = 3×5. Taking the highest power of each prime: LCM = 2 × 3 × 5 = 30.

distractors.A: 24 isn't divisible by 10 or 15.
distractors.C: 60 is a common multiple but not the least.
distractors.D: 90 is a common multiple but not the least.

questions.stem: A certain amount of money is shared among Jay, Troy and Jordan in the ratio 2:3:9. If Troy received $40 more than Jay, then the amount of money shared is
questions.explanation: Troy gets 3 parts and Jay gets 2 parts, so the difference is 1 part = $40. The total is 2 + 3 + 9 = 14 parts, so 14 × $40 = $560.

distractors.A: $280 = 7 × $40 — that's only half the total parts.
distractors.B: $360 = 9 × $40 — that's just Jordan's share, not the total.
distractors.C: $400 = 10 × $40 — wrong number of parts.

questions.explanation: The union collects every element appearing in any of the sets — list each one once. Combining gives {2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7}.

distractors.A: {2} would be the intersection P ∩ Q ∩ S, not the union.
distractors.B: {2, 3} appears in P and Q only — missing elements unique to S.
distractors.C: {2, 3, 5} misses 4, 6 and 7.

questions.stem: The shaded region in the Venn diagram represents
questions.explanation: The shading covers the universal set outside both circles — every element that is neither in P nor in Q. That is the complement of the union, written (P ∪ Q)'.

distractors.A: P ∪ Q' would shade all of P plus everything outside Q.
distractors.B: (P ∩ Q)' would shade everything except the lens-shaped overlap.
distractors.C: Q ∩ P' would shade only the part of Q outside P.

diagram.alt: Venn diagram showing sets P and Q with shaded region

questions.stem: The elements of the set {x : 6 ≤ x < 10, where x is an integer} are
questions.explanation: The condition 6 ≤ x < 10 includes 6 (≤) but excludes 10 (<). The integers satisfying this are 6, 7, 8 and 9.

distractors.A: Drops the 6 — but 6 ≤ x means 6 is included.
distractors.C: Includes 10 — but x < 10 excludes 10.
distractors.D: Wrongly includes both endpoints.

questions.stem: M = {p, q, r} and N = {p, q}. Which of the following statements about sets M and N is true?
questions.explanation: Every element of N is already in M, so N is a subset of M. Therefore M ∪ N just gives back M.

distractors.A: It's the other way round: N ⊂ M, not M ⊂ N.
distractors.C: M ∪ N = N would only be true if M ⊂ N, but r is not in N.
distractors.D: M ∩ N = {p, q}, not the empty set.

questions.stem: All students in a class play Scrabble or Checkers or both. If 36% of the students play Scrabble only and 48% of the students play Checkers only, what percentage of students play BOTH games?
questions.explanation: Since every student plays at least one game, the three regions (Scrabble only, Checkers only, both) sum to 100%. Both = 100 − 36 − 48 = 16%.

distractors.A: 12% comes from 100 − 36 − 52 or other arithmetic slips.
distractors.C: 84% = 36 + 48; that's the total who play just one game, not both.
distractors.D: 88% mistakes 'both' for the union of all three regions.

questions.stem: The diagram below shows the universal set U and two sets, T and C, that represent the students in a class who play tennis (T) and chess (C). How many students play tennis but NOT chess?
questions.explanation: "Tennis but NOT chess" means inside the T circle but OUTSIDE the C circle. From the diagram those are Aly, Alex, Tom and Kayla — 4 students. (Paul and Rema are inside C, so excluded; Dora, Doug and Chaz are outside T altogether.)

distractors.A: Two is too few — count Aly, Alex, Tom AND Kayla.
distractors.B: Close, but four — there are four names between the T and C circles.
distractors.D: Six counts every student in T (including Paul + Rema inside C). The question excludes chess-players.

diagram.alt: Venn diagram with named students placed in tennis (T) and chess (C) sets

questions.stem: A dress which costs $180.00 is being sold at a discount of 10%. The amount of the discount is
questions.explanation: 10% of $180 = $180 × 0.10 = $18.00. That is the discount, not the sale price.

distractors.A: $1.80 is 1% of $180 — decimal slipped two places.
distractors.B: $10 ignores the $180 and just takes 10 from the percent figure.
distractors.D: $170 is the sale price ($180 − $10 mis-discount), not the discount itself.

questions.stem: If EC$2.50 is equivalent to US$1.00, then EC$25.00, in US dollars, is
questions.explanation: Divide by the exchange rate: EC$25.00 ÷ 2.50 = US$10.00.

distractors.A: $5 is EC$25 ÷ 5 — wrong rate.
distractors.C: $62.50 = 25 × 2.50, multiplying instead of dividing.
distractors.D: $80 has no clear basis.

questions.stem: For every sale amounting to $1, a salesman is paid $0.10 as commission. If his sales for a particular month was $1 020.00, how much commission was he paid?
questions.explanation: Commission = 10% of sales = $1 020.00 × 0.10 = $102.00.

distractors.A: $10.20 is 1% of sales — decimal slip.
distractors.B: $20.20 has no clear basis.
distractors.D: $110 is just an over-rounded estimate.

questions.stem: A man's basic wage for a 40-hour week is $160.00. He is paid overtime at the rate of 1¼ times the hourly rate. If he works 6½ hours overtime in a certain week, his wage for that week is
questions.explanation: Hourly rate = $160 ÷ 40 = $4. Overtime rate = $4 × 1.25 = $5/hr. Overtime pay = $5 × 6.5 = $32.50. Total wage = $160 + $32.50 = $192.50.

distractors.A: $165 ignores the 1¼ multiplier on overtime hours.
distractors.B: $166.50 underweights the overtime rate.
distractors.C: $171.50 uses the basic rate for overtime hours.

questions.stem: A television set costs $350 cash. When bought on hire purchase, a deposit of $35 is required, followed by 12 monthly payments of $30 each. How much money is saved by paying cash?
questions.explanation: Hire purchase total = $35 deposit + 12 × $30 = $35 + $360 = $395. Saving = $395 − $350 = $45.

distractors.A: $10 ignores the deposit entirely.
distractors.B: $25 misses one of the instalments.
distractors.C: $40 comes from $360 + $30 − $350 (mis-counting).

questions.stem: At the end of any year, a car is worth 5% less than what it was worth at the beginning of the year. If a car was worth $9 500 in December 2021, then its value in January 2021 was
questions.explanation: December value = 95% of January value, so January value × 0.95 = $9 500. January value = $9 500 ÷ 0.95 = $10 000.

distractors.A: $9 025 = 0.95 × $9 500 — depreciating again instead of working back.
distractors.B: $9 995 = $9 500 × 1.05 — uses 5% increase incorrectly.
distractors.D: $10 025 ≈ $9 500 × 1.0553, an off-by approximation.

questions.stem: A loan of $15 000 was repaid in 24 equal monthly instalments of $750. The total interest paid as a percentage of the principal was
questions.explanation: Total repaid = 24 × $750 = $18 000. Interest paid = $18 000 − $15 000 = $3 000. As a percentage of principal: \(\dfrac{3000}{15000}\times 100\% = 20\%\).

distractors.A: 5% would mean only $750 in interest — too low.
distractors.B: \(8\tfrac{1}{3}\%\) is the simple-interest rate annualised, not the total interest paid.
distractors.C: \(16\tfrac{2}{3}\%\) is similar — a per-annum rate, not the total over the 2 years.

questions.stem: At a sale, a discount of $3.00 off the original price was offered for each book. Daniel paid $46.00 for 2 books that had the same sale price. What was the original cost of ONE of his books?
questions.explanation: Sale price per book = $46 ÷ 2 = $23. Original price = sale price + discount = $23 + $3 = $26.

distractors.A: $20 = $23 − $3 — subtracted the discount instead of adding it back.
distractors.B: $21.50 doesn't match either direction of the calculation.
distractors.C: $24.50 = $23 + $1.50 — applied half the discount.

questions.stem: 3x² × 2x³ =
questions.explanation: Multiply coefficients (3 × 2 = 6) and add the indices (x² × x³ = x⁵). Result: 6x⁵.

distractors.B: Adds the coefficients (3 + 2 = 5) instead of multiplying.
distractors.C: Multiplies the indices (2 × 3 = 6) instead of adding.
distractors.D: Both errors at once: 3 × 2 × 12 — nonsense combination.

questions.stem: If 5(y − 2) = 3(y + 4), then y is
questions.explanation: Expanding: 5y − 10 = 3y + 12. Collect: 2y = 22, so y = 11.

distractors.A: 1 doesn't satisfy either side.
distractors.B: 3 makes 5(1) = 5 and 3(7) = 21 — not equal.
distractors.C: 7 makes 5(5) = 25 and 3(11) = 33 — not equal.

questions.stem: Seven times the product of two numbers, a and b, may be written as
questions.explanation: 'Product of a and b' = ab. 'Seven times' that = 7ab.

distractors.A: ab/7 is the product divided by 7, not multiplied.
distractors.C: 7a + b applies 7 only to a, not the whole product.
distractors.D: 7(a + b) uses the sum, not the product.

questions.stem: Given that 3 * 6 = 3 and 2 * 4 = 0, then a * b may be defined as
questions.explanation: Test option A on each pair: 3² − 6 = 9 − 6 = 3 ✓ and 2² − 4 = 4 − 4 = 0 ✓. Both work, so a * b = a² − b.

questions.stem: If \(x = -2\), \(y = 3\), \(t = 3\), then \(\left(\dfrac{x}{y}\right)^{t} = \)
questions.explanation: \(\left(\dfrac{-2}{3}\right)^{3} = \dfrac{(-2)^3}{3^3} = \dfrac{-8}{27}\). The negative cubes to negative.

distractors.A: You multiplied instead of cubing.
distractors.C: Forgot the negative sign — odd power preserves sign.
distractors.D: You inverted the fraction.

questions.stem: If \(\dfrac{1}{3^{2x}} = 9\), then the value of \(x\) is
questions.explanation: Rewrite: 1/32x = 3-2x. So 3-2x = 9 = 3². Equating powers: -2x = 2 → x = -1.

distractors.B: x = 1 gives 1/9, not 9.
distractors.C: x = 2 gives 1/81.
distractors.D: x = 1½ gives 1/27.

questions.stem: Given that A = [[1, 3, -3], [3, 0, 5]], then 3A equals
questions.explanation: To multiply a matrix by a scalar, multiply every entry by 3: 3·1=3, 3·3=9, 3·(-3)=-9, 3·3=9, 3·0=0, 3·5=15.

distractors.B: Adds 3 to each entry instead of multiplying.
distractors.C: Forgets to multiply the third entry of row 1.
distractors.D: Mixes scalar multiplication with subtraction.

questions.stem: The determinant of the 2 × 2 identity matrix is
questions.explanation: The 2×2 identity matrix is [[1, 0], [0, 1]]. Determinant = (1)(1) − (0)(0) = 1.

distractors.A: Zero would mean the matrix is singular (no inverse).
distractors.C: Determinants of square matrices are always defined.
distractors.D: Sign error — the diagonal product is +1, not -1.

questions.stem: Given p = (3, 7) and q = (-2, 5) (as column vectors), the vector p − q is represented by
questions.explanation: Subtract component by component: (3 − (−2), 7 − 5) = (5, 2).

distractors.A: (1, 12) adds the vectors instead of subtracting.
distractors.B: (1, 2) uses 3 − 2 (forgetting the negative on q's first entry).
distractors.D: (5, 12) mixes subtraction in one component and addition in the other.

questions.stem: In parallelogram WXYZ, XY = u and XW = v. R is the midpoint of YZ. RW, expressed in terms of u and v, is
questions.explanation: YZ is parallel to XW so YZ = v. R is the midpoint of YZ, so YR = ½v. From R go to Y then to W: RW = -½v + YW. Since YW = -u + v, we get RW = -½v + (-u + v) = ½v − u.

distractors.B: u + ½v has the wrong sign on u (heading the wrong way).
distractors.C: u − ½v reverses the v component as well.
distractors.D: -½v − u has the wrong sign on the v component.

diagram.alt: Parallelogram WXYZ with vectors u (XY) and v (XW), R as midpoint of YZ

questions.stem: Given that 1 millimetre = 1/1000 metres, then 2 500 millimetres, in metres, is
questions.explanation: Divide by 1 000 to convert mm to m: 2 500 ÷ 1 000 = 2.5 m.

distractors.A: 0.25 divides by 10 000 (one zero too many).
distractors.C: 25.0 divides by only 100.
distractors.D: 250 divides by only 10.

questions.stem: The length of the edge of a cube that has a volume of 27 cm³ is
questions.explanation: Volume of a cube = side³, so side = ∛27 = 3 cm.

distractors.B: 4.5 cm has no clear basis.
distractors.C: 6 cm comes from halving 27 ÷ 4.5 — wrong operation.
distractors.D: 9 cm gives a volume of 729 cm³.

questions.stem: The perimeter of a square is 48 cm. What is its area, in cm²?
questions.explanation: Side length = 48 ÷ 4 = 12 cm. Area = 12² = 144 cm².

distractors.A: 36 = 6², from a side of 6 instead of 12.
distractors.B: 72 has no consistent derivation.
distractors.C: 108 doesn't match either.

questions.stem: A trapezium PQRS has parallel sides 8 cm and 18 cm and an area of 65 cm². The height, h, of PQRS is
questions.explanation: Area = ½(a + b)h, so 65 = ½(8 + 18)h = 13h. Therefore h = 65 ÷ 13 = 5 cm.

distractors.A: 2.5 cm halves the correct answer (forgot the ½ factor cancels).
distractors.B: 3.6 cm comes from 65 ÷ 18, using only one parallel side.
distractors.D: 8.0 cm comes from 65 ÷ 8, using only the other parallel side.

diagram.alt: Trapezium PQRS with parallel sides 8 cm and 18 cm

questions.stem: A sector \(AOB\) of a circle has angle \(AOB = 60°\) and radius \(OB = r\) units. The length of the arc \(AB\) is
questions.explanation: Arc length \(= \dfrac{\theta}{360°} \times 2\pi r = \dfrac{60}{360}\times 2\pi r = \dfrac{1}{3}\pi r\).

distractors.A: \(\dfrac{\pi r}{6}\) uses 30° instead of 60° (or forgets to double).
distractors.C: \(\dfrac{\pi r}{2}\) would correspond to 90°, not 60°.
distractors.D: \(\pi r\) would correspond to 180° — half the circumference.

diagram.alt: Sector AOB of a circle with angle 60° and radius r

questions.stem: A compound shape is made up of a rectangle (width 15 cm) and two identical semi-circles, one on either of the short sides. The total length of the shape is 27 cm. The area of the compound shape, in terms of π, is
questions.explanation: Two semicircles' diameter = 27 − 15 = 12, so radius = 6 (this is also the height of the rectangle). Rectangle = 15 × 12 = 180. Two semicircles = full circle = π(6)² = 36π. Total = 180 + 36π = 36(5 + π).

distractors.A: 6(9 + π) uses the wrong dimensions for the rectangle.
distractors.C: 6(5 + 2π) misuses the radius and circle area.
distractors.D: 18(5 + 2π) doubles up the circle area incorrectly.

diagram.alt: Compound shape: rectangle of width 15 cm with two identical semicircles

questions.subtopic: Effect on area of changing dimensions
questions.stem: The area of a rectangle is 40 cm². If the length is multiplied by 4 and the width is halved, the area would then be
questions.explanation: New area = (4L) × (W/2) = 2 × (LW) = 2 × 40 = 80 cm².

distractors.A: 20 cm² halves the area (only the width change applied).
distractors.B: 40 cm² assumes the changes cancel exactly — they don't.
distractors.D: 160 cm² multiplies by 4 only (ignores the halving).

questions.stem: A compound shape comprises a triangle resting on a square of side 5 cm. If the TOTAL area of the compound shape is 35 cm², then the height of the triangle is
questions.explanation: Square area = 5 × 5 = 25 cm². Triangle area = 35 − 25 = 10 cm². With base 5: ½ × 5 × h = 10, so h = 4 cm.

distractors.A: 2 cm forgets the ½ factor in the triangle's area formula.
distractors.C: 5 cm uses 25 ÷ 5 = 5, ignoring the ½.
distractors.D: 10 cm uses the wrong area for the triangle.

diagram.alt: Compound shape: triangle resting on a square of side 5 cm

questions.stem: A frequency distribution gives mass classes 21–30 (28), 31–40 (34), 41–50 (12), 51–60 (38), 61–70 (20). The number of children who have a mass of at least 41 kg is
questions.explanation: 'At least 41 kg' = sum of the last three classes: 12 + 38 + 20 = 70.

distractors.A: 12 takes only the 41–50 class.
distractors.B: 62 = 28 + 34, the count below 41 kg.
distractors.D: 104 adds another class by mistake.

questions.stem: Using the same frequency distribution as in the previous item — mass classes 21–30 (28), 31–40 (34), 41–50 (12), 51–60 (38), 61–70 (20) — the lower limit (boundary) of the modal class is
questions.explanation: The modal class has the highest frequency, which is 38 in the 51–60 class. The lower class boundary is 50.5 (halfway between 50 and 51).

distractors.A: 30.5 is the lower boundary of the 31–40 class.
distractors.B: 41.0 is the lower limit of 41–50, not the modal class.
distractors.D: 51.0 is the lower limit, not the lower boundary.

questions.stem: Ms Clarke arranged the 15 test scores of her students in order of size and selected the 8th score for reporting purposes. Which of the following statistical measures did Ms Clarke obtain?
questions.explanation: For 15 ordered values, the middle position is the (15+1)/2 = 8th. The middle value is the median.

distractors.A: Mean would require summing and dividing.
distractors.B: Mode is the most frequent value, not the middle one.
distractors.C: Range is highest minus lowest.

questions.stem: A pie chart, drawn to scale, shows how a student used 12 hours studying English (E), Mathematics (M), French (F) and Geography (G). The amount of time spent studying Mathematics is APPROXIMATELY
questions.explanation: The Mathematics sector occupies roughly a quarter of the pie (≈ 90°). One-quarter of 12 hours is 3 hours.

distractors.A: 1 hour would be a 30° sliver — much smaller than what's shown.
distractors.B: 2 hours = 60° — still smaller than the M sector.
distractors.D: 4 hours = 120° — bigger than the M sector.

diagram.alt: Pie chart showing 12 hours studying English, Maths and other subjects

questions.subtopic: Finding a missing value from the mean
questions.stem: If the mean of the FOUR numbers 4, 8, x and 12 is 10, then x is
questions.explanation: Sum of the four numbers = 4 × 10 = 40. So 4 + 8 + x + 12 = 40, giving x = 16.

distractors.A: 4 gives a sum of 28 (mean 7).
distractors.B: 10 gives a sum of 34 (mean 8.5).
distractors.C: 12 gives a sum of 36 (mean 9).

questions.stem: A two-way table records 100 respondents by ethnicity (P or R) and attitude towards Mathematics (Positive, Neutral, Negative). The cells are P–Positive 25, R–Positive 12; P–Neutral 11, R–Neutral 9; P–Negative 24, R–Negative 19. A respondent is selected at random. What is the probability that the selected respondent is of ethnicity P and has a positive attitude towards Mathematics?
questions.explanation: Required outcomes = 25 (cell where P meets Positive). Total outcomes = 100. P(P and Positive) = 25/100 = 0.25.

distractors.B: 0.37 is the probability of being Positive overall (37/100).
distractors.C: 0.42 doesn't match any clean count.
distractors.D: 0.60 is the probability of being ethnicity P (60/100).

questions.stem: Which of the following diagrams BEST illustrates a function? (diagrams in source paper)

options.A: Mapping where one input is linked to several outputs
options.B: Mapping where each input has exactly one output (one-to-one)
options.C: Mapping where one input has two outputs
options.D: Mapping where two inputs share an output but one input also branches

questions.explanation: A function assigns exactly one output to each input. The one-to-one mapping in option B satisfies this without ambiguity. Options A and C show inputs with multiple outputs, which violates the definition.

distractors.A: An input mapping to several outputs is not a function.
distractors.C: Same problem — one input gives more than one output.
distractors.D: Branching from any single input also breaks the function rule.

questions.stem: The graph below shows a straight line AB. The point where the line AB crosses the horizontal axis is
questions.explanation: The line crosses the horizontal (x) axis where y = 0. From the graph that point is (3, 0).

distractors.B: (0, 3) is on the y-axis, not the x-axis.
distractors.C: (0, -2) is the y-intercept (the other end of segment AB).
distractors.D: (3, -2) isn't on either axis.

diagram.alt: Line AB on a coordinate plane — passes through A(0, -2) and B(3, 0)

questions.stem: For the same straight line AB shown in the previous item, the equation of AB is
questions.explanation: Gradient = (0 − (−2)) / (3 − 0) = 2/3. The y-intercept is −2. So y = (2/3)x − 2.

distractors.A: Slope of 1 wouldn't pass through both A and B.
distractors.B: Slope of 2 puts B at y = 4 instead of 0.
distractors.D: Negative slope would slope the wrong way.

diagram.alt: Line AB on a coordinate plane — passes through A(0, -2) and B(3, 0)

questions.stem: The coordinates of the maximum point of the function y = 4x − x² are
questions.explanation: Complete the square: y = -(x² − 4x) = -(x − 2)² + 4. Maximum occurs at x = 2, giving y = 4. So max point is (2, 4).

distractors.B: (0, 4) is the y-intercept value but x = 0 gives y = 0, not 4.
distractors.C: (4, 2) swaps the coordinates.
distractors.D: (4, 4) plugs x = 4 to get y = 0, not 4.

questions.stem: The equation of the axis of symmetry of the quadratic function y = 4x − x² is
questions.explanation: The axis of symmetry passes vertically through the maximum point (2, 4). Its equation is x = 2.

distractors.B: y = 2 is a horizontal line — axis of symmetry of a parabola is vertical.
distractors.C: y = 4 is the horizontal line through the vertex, not the axis.
distractors.D: y = 2x isn't even a constant line.

distractors.B: -1 comes from forgetting the minus on 14 − 3 step.
distractors.C: 1 ignores the negative entirely.
distractors.D: 13/5 = (14 − 3)/5 — drops the negative.

questions.stem: A parking lot charges $1.00 plus $0.25 for every hour or part thereof (h) in which a vehicle is parked. A function, C(h), that represents the parking cost is
questions.explanation: Fixed charge $1.00 + variable charge $0.25 per hour: C(h) = 0.25h + 1.00.

distractors.A: 0.25h ignores the flat fee.
distractors.B: Swaps the roles of the per-hour and flat charges.
distractors.D: Dividing by 0.25 doesn't represent a per-hour charge.

questions.stem: The range of f: x → x³ for the domain {-2, -1, 0, 1, 2} is
questions.explanation: Cube each input: (-2)³ = -8, (-1)³ = -1, 0³ = 0, 1³ = 1, 2³ = 8. Range = {-8, -1, 0, 1, 8}.

distractors.A: Treats x³ like x² (loses the negatives).
distractors.B: Multiplies inputs by 3 instead of cubing.
distractors.C: Cube of -1 is -1, not -3 (and so on).

questions.stem: If the sum of the interior angles of a polygon is 4 right angles, then the polygon is a
questions.explanation: 4 right angles = 360°. Using (n − 2)·180° = 360° gives n − 2 = 2, so n = 4 (quadrilateral).

distractors.A: Triangle's angles sum to 180° (2 right angles).
distractors.B: Hexagon sums to 720° (8 right angles).
distractors.C: Pentagon sums to 540° (6 right angles).

questions.stem: In a right-angled triangle with right angle at B, BC = 4 cm (vertical leg) and CA = 8 cm (hypotenuse). Angle x is at A and angle y is at C. Which trigonometric ratio is equal to 4/8?
questions.explanation: From angle x (at vertex A): the side opposite is BC = 4 and the hypotenuse is CA = 8. So sin x = opposite/hypotenuse = 4/8.

distractors.B: Tan y uses opposite/adjacent at C — neither side here is 8.
distractors.C: Sin y = opposite/hyp = AB/8, where AB ≠ 4.
distractors.D: Tan x = opposite/adjacent = 4/AB, not 4/8.

diagram.alt: Right-angled triangle with right angle at B, BC = 4 cm, CA = 8 cm

questions.stem: In an isosceles triangle the two base angles are each 30°. The apex angle x° is
questions.explanation: Angles in a triangle sum to 180°. So x = 180 − 30 − 30 = 120°.

distractors.A: 30° matches a base angle, not the apex.
distractors.B: 60° would mean an equilateral triangle (all angles 60°).
distractors.D: 150° leaves only 30° for both base angles together.

questions.stem: Two right-angled triangles are similar. Triangle ABC has angle 31° at A, AB = 6 cm and hypotenuse AC = 7 cm. Triangle MNO has angle 31° at M and MN = 3 cm. The length of MO, in cm, is
questions.explanation: The triangles are similar with scale factor MN/AB = 3/6 = ½. MO corresponds to AC, so MO = 7 × ½ = 3.5 cm.

distractors.A: 2.5 doesn't match the scale factor.
distractors.B: 3.0 just copies MN.
distractors.D: 7.0 keeps the original length, ignoring the scale.

questions.stem: A(2, 5) is mapped onto A'(4, 6) and B(5, 7) is mapped onto B'(7, 8). The translation vector is
questions.explanation: Subtract the position of A from A' (or B from B'): (4 − 2, 6 − 5) = (2, 1). Same vector applies to B → B'.

distractors.B: (1, 1) would need A' at (3, 6).
distractors.C: (3, 2) would shift A to (5, 7).
distractors.D: (5, 3) would shift A to (7, 8).

questions.stem: OAA', OBB' and OCC' are straight lines. Triangle ABC is mapped onto triangle A'B'C' by an enlargement with centre O. What is the scale factor of the enlargement? (See the diagram below.)
questions.explanation: The image is on the same side of O as the object and is twice as far away, so the scale factor is +2.

distractors.A: Negative would flip the image to the other side of O.
distractors.B: -½ flips and shrinks — the image here is larger.
distractors.C: +½ would shrink the image; here it's enlarged.

questions.stem: The angle of depression of a point G from E is 30°. F is directly below E with EF = 25 m, and FG is horizontal (right angle at F). The distance FG, in metres, is
questions.explanation: In right triangle EFG (right angle at F), the angle at E equals the angle of depression's complement = 60°. tan(60°) = FG/25, so FG = 25 tan 60°. Since tan 60° = 1/tan 30°, this equals 25 / tan 30°.

distractors.B: 25/tan 60° = 25/√3, which is the height divided by tan 60° — wrong angle used.
distractors.C: 25 cos 30° gives the adjacent side from a different triangle setup.
distractors.D: 25 sin 30° = 12.5 — too short for FG.

diagram.alt: Angle of depression of point G from E with F directly below E, EF = 25 m

questions.stem: A surveyor sets up his instrument 12 metres from the foot of a building. The instrument is 1.6 m above the ground and the angle of elevation to the top of the building is 40°. An estimate of the height of the building can be obtained by calculating
questions.explanation: From instrument level: rise of building above instrument = 12 tan 40°. The instrument itself is 1.6 m above the ground, so total height = 1.6 + 12 tan 40°.

distractors.B: Cosine gives the adjacent side, not the vertical rise.
distractors.C: Subtracting 1.6 ignores that the instrument adds height to the ground level.
distractors.D: Sine on the adjacent leg is the wrong ratio entirely.

diagram.alt: Surveyor with instrument 12 m from building, instrument height above ground

questions.stem: What percentage of 40 is 8?

distractors.A: \(5\%\) comes from \(\frac{8}{40}\) written as a decimal (0.2) but treated as 5 — possibly \(\frac{40}{8}\) inverted incorrectly.
distractors.C: \(32\%\) comes from \(40-8=32\), taking the difference rather than the percentage.
distractors.D: \(150\%\) flips the question to \(\frac{40}{8}\times 100\%\), which would ask 'what percentage of 8 is 40' — different question.

questions.stem: The value of the digit 2 in the number 748.621 is
questions.explanation: In 748.621, the digit 2 sits in the hundredths place — the second position after the decimal point. Its value is \(\dfrac{2}{100} = \dfrac{2}{10^{2}}\).

distractors.B: \(\dfrac{2}{10^{3}} = 0.002\) — that's the thousandths place, where the 1 sits.
distractors.C: \(2\times 10^{-1} = 0.2\) — the tenths place, where the 6 sits.
distractors.D: \(2000\times 10^{-3} = 2\) — that would put the digit in the units column.

distractors.A: $1 would come from \(\sqrt{17-15}\cdot\sqrt{17+15}\) taken as \(\sqrt{2-2}\) — sign mistake.
distractors.B: $2 is just $17-15, forgetting the squaring and the square root.
distractors.D: $16 would be \(\sqrt{256}\) — perhaps \(17^{2}-15^{2}\) mis-evaluated as $32 then doubled.

questions.stem: In a school, the ratio of the number of pupils to the number of teachers is 20 : 1. If the number of pupils is 840, how many teachers are there?
questions.explanation: For every 20 pupils there is 1 teacher. Number of teachers \(= \dfrac{840}{20} = 42\).

distractors.A: $40 would be \(\frac{800}{20}\) — dropping the 40 extra pupils.
distractors.C: $820 comes from $840-20, treating the ratio as a subtraction.
distractors.D: $840 is the number of pupils, not teachers.

questions.stem: \(99\times 101\) has the same value as
questions.explanation: Use the distributive law: \(99\times 101 = 99\times(100+1) = 99\times 100 + 99\times 1\).

distractors.B: Multiplying these two products gives a huge number — distributive law uses addition, not multiplication of the parts.
distractors.C: Subtraction would give \(99\times(100-1) = 99\times 99\), not \(99\times 101\).

questions.stem: Which of the following sets is defined by \(\{x\in \mathbb{Z}: -2\le x\le 4\}\)?
questions.explanation: Both endpoints are included (\(\le\)). The integers from \(-2\) to $4 inclusive are \(-2, -1, 0, 1, 2, 3, 4\).

distractors.A: Drops the negatives and the 0 — only positive integers from 1 to 4.
distractors.B: Misses the negative values \(-2\) and \(-1\).
distractors.C: Misses the endpoints \(-2\) and $4, treating the inequality as strict.

questions.stem: In which of the following pairs of sets is one set NOT a subset of the other?

options.D: G = {multiples of five} and H = {multiples of ten}

questions.explanation:
Check each pair:

  • (A) {multiples of 12} = {12, 24, 36, ...} and {factors of 8} = {1, 2, 4, 8}. Neither is a subset of the other (no common elements).
  • (B) {factors of 6} = {1, 2, 3, 6} ⊂ {factors of 12} = {1, 2, 3, 4, 6, 12}.
  • (C) Every whole number is a rational number, so {whole numbers} ⊂ {rational numbers}.
  • (D) Every multiple of 10 is also a multiple of 5, so {multiples of 10} ⊂ {multiples of 5}.

Only pair A has neither set being a subset of the other.

distractors.B: {factors of 6} ⊂ {factors of 12} since every factor of 6 is also a factor of 12.
distractors.C: Whole numbers are a subset of rational numbers (any integer can be written as a fraction).
distractors.D: Multiples of 10 are a subset of multiples of 5.

questions.stem: All students in a class play Scrabble or Checkers or both. Sixteen per cent of the students play BOTH Scrabble and Checkers. The number of students who play Checkers only is TWICE the number of students who play Scrabble only. What percentage of students play Checkers but NOT Scrabble?
questions.explanation: Let Scrabble only = \(s\). Then Checkers only = \(2s\). Since everyone plays at least one game: \(s + 2s + 16 = 100\), so \(3s = 84\) and \(s = 28\). Therefore Checkers only \(= 2(28) = 56\%\).

distractors.A: \(28\%\) is Scrabble only (not what was asked).
distractors.B: \(32\%\) doesn't match a valid split.
distractors.D: \(72\%\) is the total who play Checkers (both + Checkers only = \(16 + 56 = 72\)), which is more than just 'Checkers but NOT Scrabble'.

questions.stem: The diagram below shows a Venn diagram where U = {students who play games}, H = {students who play hockey}, V = {students who play volleyball}, with the number of students in each region indicated. How many students play EITHER hockey OR volleyball but NOT both?
questions.explanation: 'EITHER hockey OR volleyball but NOT both' is the symmetric difference: count the students in H only plus the students in V only (excluding the overlap). From the diagram this gives 9.

distractors.A: $6 counts only one of the two 'only' regions.
distractors.B: $7 misses one student from a region.
distractors.D: $11 adds the intersection to one of the 'only' regions.

diagram.alt: Venn diagram for hockey and volleyball with student counts

questions.stem: The set of positive integers that are divisible by 7 is an example of
questions.explanation: The positive integers divisible by 7 are 7, 14, 21, 28, 35, … — they go on forever. A set with no upper limit on its members is an infinite set.

distractors.A: "Improper set" is not a standard term in CSEC set theory.
distractors.C: An empty set has no elements; this set has many.
distractors.D: A finite set has a fixed number of members; this one keeps growing.

questions.stem: A shopping bill shows: 4 kg flour at unit price P with total $12.40; 2 kg sugar at $3.60 per kg with total $7.20; 8 kg rice at unit price Q with total T; subtotal $40.40. The correct values of P, Q and T are
questions.explanation: P (unit price of flour) = $12.40 ÷ 4 = $3.10. Subtotal of rice = $40.40 − $12.40 − $7.20 = $20.80, so T = $20.80. Q (unit price of rice) = $20.80 ÷ 8 = $2.60.

distractors.A: These values don't add up to the subtotal of $40.40.
distractors.B: \(Q = \$6.70 would give rice total \(= 8\times 6.70 = \$53.60, way over.
distractors.C: \(P = \$4.05 would make flour total \(= \$16.20, not \(\$12.40.

diagram.alt: Shopping bill table — items 13 and 14 share this diagram

questions.stem: According to the bill (subtotal $40.40), the amount paid for VAT (8%) is

distractors.A: \(\$3.10 is the unit price of flour, not the VAT.
distractors.C: \(\$37.17 is roughly $40.40 - 3.23 — the post-VAT subtotal.
distractors.D: \(\$43.63 is the total including VAT, not the VAT itself.

questions.stem: At a bank, EC$2.60 is equivalent to US$1.00. For every US$1.00 exchanged, EC$0.10 is deducted as an exchange tax. How many EC dollars will Leon receive if he exchanges US$1 000.00?
questions.explanation: Gross EC dollars = 1000 × 2.60 = $2 600. Exchange tax = 1000 × 0.10 = $100. Leon receives $2 600 − $100 = $2 500.

distractors.A: \(\$900.90 doesn't match any clean calculation.
distractors.B: \(\\(2\,360.34\) comes from a wrong tax application.
distractors.D: \(\\(2\,600\) ignores the exchange tax entirely.

questions.stem: A calculator which is marked at $120 is sold for cash at a 30% discount. How much change would Susan receive if she pays for the calculator with a $100 bill?

distractors.B: \(\$20 would assume the discount is \(\$20 off (not \(30\%\)).
distractors.C: $\$28$ might come from forgetting to subtract from $100.
distractors.D: \(\$36 is \(30\%\) of \(\$120 — the discount itself, not the change.

questions.stem: By selling a bag for $1 140, Vishal incurred a loss of 5%. At what price should he have sold the bag to gain a profit of 5%?

distractors.A: \(\\(1\,197 = 1140\times 1.05\) — applies \(5\%\) on the loss-making price, not the cost price.
distractors.B: \(\\(1\,254 = 1140 + (1140\times 0.10)\) — wrong base for the percentage.
distractors.C: \(\\(1\,256.85\) doesn't match any clean derivation.

questions.stem: The cost of a machine is estimated to be decreasing at the rate of 10% every year. If it currently costs $6 000, what will be the estimated value of the machine after 2 years?
questions.explanation: Each year the value is multiplied by $0.90. After 2 years: $6000\times 0.90^{2} = 6000\times 0.81 = \\(4\,860\).

distractors.A: \(\\(1\,140\) is far too low — applies depreciation incorrectly.
distractors.B: \(\\(4\,800 = 6000\times 0.80\) — combines the two years' losses as \(20\%\) once.
distractors.D: \(\\(5\,400 = 6000\times 0.90\) — only one year's depreciation.

questions.stem: A man pays 60 cents for every 200 m³ of gas used, plus a fixed charge. If he pays $178.75 when he uses 55 000 m³ of gas, how much is the fixed charge?

distractors.B: \(\$14.35 doesn't match the variable subtraction.
distractors.C: \(\$151.25 is $178.75 - 27.50 — wrong variable rate.
distractors.D: \(\$165.00 is the variable charge, not the fixed charge.

questions.stem: The compound interest on $12 000 for 2 years at 10% per annum, compounded annually, is

distractors.A: \(\\(1\,320\) is one year's interest only.
distractors.C: \(\\(13\,200 = 12\,000\times 1.10\) — the amount after one year.
distractors.D: \(\\(14\,520\) is the final amount, not just the interest.

questions.stem: The product of a number \(p\) and its reciprocal may be written as
questions.explanation: The reciprocal of \(p\) is \(\dfrac{1}{p}\). The product of \(p\) and its reciprocal is \(p\times \dfrac{1}{p} = 1\).

distractors.A: \(p^{2}\times (-p) = -p^{3}\) — not a reciprocal product.
distractors.B: \(p\times (-p) = -p^{2}\) — uses the negative, not the reciprocal.
distractors.C: \(p^{2}\times \dfrac{1}{p} = p\) — uses the reciprocal but wrong first factor.

questions.stem: A rectangle has width 2 cm and length \((x-4)\) cm. The area of the rectangle, in cm², is \(x^{2}\). The equation that may be used to find the value of \(x\) is
questions.explanation: Area of rectangle \(=\) length \(\times\) width \(= (x-4)\times 2 = 2(x-4)\). Setting this equal to \(x^{2}\) gives \(x^{2} = 2(x-4)\).

distractors.B: Treats both dimensions as expressions \((x-2)\) and \((x-4)\) — but the width is just 2.
distractors.C: \((x-4)(x+2)\) uses an unrelated factorisation.
distractors.D: Multiplies all three pieces — there's no third dimension.

questions.stem: Althea, Bob and Chris collect shells. Althea has \(p\) shells, Bob has TWICE as many shells as Althea, and Chris has 4 more than the total number of shells that both Althea and Bob collected. Altogether, the total number of shells they have is

distractors.A: \(6p\) misses the constant \(+4\) from Chris's count.
distractors.B: \(3p+4\) is just Chris's count, not the total.
distractors.C: \(6(p+2) = 6p+12\) — wrong constant.

questions.stem: If \(3 + \dfrac{2}{x} = 1\), then the value of \(x\) is
questions.explanation: Subtract 3: \(\dfrac{2}{x} = 1 - 3 = -2\). Multiply both sides by \(x\): \(2 = -2x\), so \(x = -1\).

questions.stem: The value of \(5^{n+1}\times 5^{n+2}\) when \(n=-1\) is

distractors.C: $10 doesn't arise from a power of 5.

questions.stem: If 40 students will take 20 days to paint a wall, how many days will it take to paint the wall if 10 more students are added, working at the same rate?
questions.explanation: This is inverse proportion: students \(\times\) days \(=\) constant. \(40\times 20 = 50\times d\), so \(d = \dfrac{800}{50} = 16\) days.

distractors.A: $12 days uses \(\frac{800}{60}\approx 13.3\) — wrong number of students.
distractors.B: $15 comes from \(\frac{800}{50}\approx 15\) rounded down — but the exact answer is 16.
distractors.D: $18 is just $20-2 for the 'extra two' students misapplied.

questions.stem: If \(P = \begin{pmatrix}2 & 5\\ -1 & 3\end{pmatrix}\), then the value of \(|P|\) is

distractors.A: $0 would mean a singular matrix.
distractors.B: $1 comes from \(6 - 5 = 1\) — sign error on the cross-product term.
distractors.C: $4 doesn't match either calculation.

questions.stem: If \(A = \begin{pmatrix}1 & 2 & 5 & 4\\ 6 & 1 & 3 & 7\\ -2 & 3 & 2 & 9\end{pmatrix}\), then the order of \(A\) is
questions.explanation: Order is rows \(\times\) columns. \(A\) has 3 rows and 4 columns, so the order is \(3\times 4\).

distractors.A: \(2\times 3\) would have 2 rows and 3 columns.
distractors.B: \(3\times 2\) swaps rows and columns and has too few columns.
distractors.D: \(4\times 3\) has the dimensions reversed.

questions.stem: The diagram below shows a parallelogram EFGH in which EF is parallel to HG, EH is parallel to FG, EF = k and EH = m. EG expressed in terms of k and m is
questions.explanation: From E, go to F (vector \(k\)), then from F to G (which is parallel to EH and equal in length, so vector \(m\)). \(\vec{EG} = \vec{EF} + \vec{FG} = k + m\).

distractors.A: \(-m - k\) has both directions reversed.
distractors.B: \(m - k\) subtracts \(k\) instead of adding.
distractors.C: \(k - m\) subtracts \(m\) instead of adding.

questions.subtopic: Scalar multiples and equality of matrices
questions.stem: If \(5\begin{pmatrix}x\\ y\end{pmatrix} = 4\begin{pmatrix}10\\ 20\end{pmatrix}\), then the values of \(x\) and \(y\) are
questions.explanation: Equating components: \(5x = 4(10) = 40\), so \(x = 8\). Similarly \(5y = 4(20) = 80\), so \(y = 16\).

distractors.A: \(x=4,\ y=5\) would require dividing by 10 and 4 respectively — wrong scalars.
distractors.C: \(x=2.5,\ y=4\) scales by \(\frac{1}{4}\) — wrong direction.
distractors.D: \(x=10,\ y=20\) ignores the factor of 5 on the left.

questions.stem: The diagram below shows a cylinder whose diameter is 6 cm and height 21 cm. Given that \(\pi = \dfrac{22}{7}\), the volume of the cylinder, in cm³, is
questions.explanation: Radius \(= 3\) cm. Volume \(= \pi r^{2}h = \dfrac{22}{7}\times 3^{2}\times 21 = \dfrac{22}{7}\times 9\times 21 = 22\times 9\times 3 = 594\) cm³.

distractors.A: $120 is too small — perhaps from \(r=2\).
distractors.B: \(396 = \pi\times 6\times 21\) — uses the diameter as \(r\) but only to power 1.
distractors.D: \(2\,376 = 4\times 594\) — uses diameter $6 as the radius.

questions.stem: In a rectangular garden plot, 15 m long and 12 m wide, an area of 80 m² is used for a vegetable garden. What area of the plot is NOT used for vegetable gardening?
questions.explanation: Total area \(= 15\times 12 = 180\) m². Area not used \(= 180 - 80 = 100\) m².

distractors.A: $26 m² takes the perimeter instead of the area.
distractors.C: $134 m² doesn't match a clean subtraction.
distractors.D: $260 m² doubles the unused area incorrectly.

questions.stem: How long will a speedboat take to travel between two harbours which are 1 080 km apart, if it travels at an average speed of 120 km h⁻¹?
questions.explanation: Time \(= \dfrac{\text{distance}}{\text{speed}} = \dfrac{1080}{120} = 9\) hours.

distractors.A: $8 hours \(\times 120 = 960\) km — short of the distance.
distractors.C: $12 hours \(\times 120 = 1440\) km — overshoots.
distractors.D: $15 hours \(\times 120 = 1800\) km — far too long.

questions.stem: The lengths of the sides of a triangle are \(x\), \(2x\) and \(2x\) centimetres. If the perimeter is 20 centimetres, what is the value of \(x\)?
questions.explanation: Perimeter: \(x + 2x + 2x = 5x = 20\). So \(x = 4\).

distractors.B: $5 would give a perimeter of $25.
distractors.C: $8 would give a perimeter of $40.
distractors.D: $10 would give a perimeter of $50.

questions.stem: The diagram below shows a trapezium with parallel sides 0.41 m (top) and 0.18 m (bottom), perpendicular height 0.12 m, and slanted side 0.21 m. The perimeter of the trapezium, in millimetres, is
questions.explanation: Perimeter (in metres) \(= 0.41 + 0.18 + 0.12 + 0.21 = 0.92\) m. Converting to millimetres: \(0.92\times 1000 = 920\) mm.

distractors.A: $0.092 would be in metres if the perimeter were $0.092 — but 0.92 m is correct.
distractors.B: $9.2 would be the perimeter in centimetres rather than mm.
distractors.C: $92 is the perimeter in cm, not mm.

questions.stem: The diagram below shows a circle, centred at O, with a major sector of area 115.5 cm² and a minor arc subtending a right angle at O. (Use \(\pi = \dfrac{22}{7}\).) The diameter of the circle, in cm, is
questions.explanation: The minor sector subtends $90°, so the major sector subtends $270° — that is \(\dfrac{3}{4}\) of the circle. So \(\dfrac{3}{4}\pi r^{2} = 115.5\), giving \(\pi r^{2} = 154\). Then \(r^{2} = 154\div \dfrac{22}{7} = 154\times \dfrac{7}{22} = 49\), so \(r = 7\) cm. Diameter \(= 14\) cm.

distractors.A: $5.5 cm has no clear basis.
distractors.B: $7.0 cm is the radius, not the diameter.
distractors.D: $28.0 cm doubles the diameter.

questions.subtopic: Surface area of a compound solid

questions.stem: The distance around a lake is 8 km. On a map, this distance around the lake is represented by a length of 4 cm. The scale on the map is

questions.stem: The table below shows the number of words that a group of 60 students got correct in a spelling test of 10 words: 6 words → 3 students, 7 words → 18 students, 8 words → 7 students, 9 words → 16 students, 10 words → 16 students. The mode of the number of words spelt correctly is
questions.explanation: The mode is the value with the highest frequency. The highest frequency is 18 (for 7 words). So the mode is 7.

questions.stem: A pie chart shows the popular games played by a group of students: Basketball 30%, Cricket 35%, Tennis (sector with central angle 36°) and Football the remainder. If 180 students play football, how many students are in the group?

distractors.A: $300 would be \(\frac{180}{0.6}\) — uses 60% as football share.

questions.stem: A bar chart shows the amount of fish bought, in kg, by the first 60 customers at a fish market. How many customers bought at LEAST 6 kg of fish?
questions.explanation: 'At least 6 kg' includes the bars at 6, 7, 8, 9 and 10 kg. Adding their heights from the bar chart gives 30 customers.

distractors.B: $19 also undercounts.
distractors.D: $34 includes too many low-weight bars.

questions.stem: The diagram below shows the cumulative frequency curve of the heights of 20 seedlings. The semi-interquartile range of the set of measurements is
questions.explanation: For 20 seedlings: \(Q_{1}\) is at the 5th value (\(\approx 1.7\) cm) and \(Q_{3}\) is at the 15th value (\(\approx 3.0\) cm). Semi-interquartile range \(= \dfrac{Q_{3}-Q_{1}}{2} = \dfrac{3.0 - 1.7}{2} = 0.65\) cm.

distractors.B: $1.30 cm is the interquartile range \(Q_{3}-Q_{1}\) itself, not the semi-IQR.

questions.stem: Six hundred students sit an examination. The probability of a randomly selected student failing the examination is \(\dfrac{1}{5}\). How many students are expected to pass the examination?
questions.explanation: P(pass) \(= 1 - \dfrac{1}{5} = \dfrac{4}{5}\). Expected to pass \(= 600\times \dfrac{4}{5} = 480\).

distractors.A: $100 doesn't match either probability.
distractors.B: $120 is the number expected to fail, not pass.
distractors.D: $500 uses \(\frac{5}{6}\) instead of \(\frac{4}{5}\).

questions.stem: Which of the following line graphs represents \(\{x : -2 < x \le 4\}\)?
questions.explanation: \(-2 < x\) means \(-2\) is excluded — open circle at \(-2\). \(x \le 4\) means $4 is included — closed circle at $4.

distractors.A: Open circle at $4 would mean \(x < 4\), not \(x \le 4\).
distractors.B: Closed circle at \(-2\) would mean \(x \ge -2\), not \(x > -2\).
distractors.C: Both endpoints are wrong: closed at \(-2\) and open at $4 both contradict the inequality.

questions.stem: Which of the following represents the equation of a straight line?
questions.explanation: A straight line has the form \(y = mx + c\), where \(m\) and \(c\) are constants. \(y = 2x + 3\) matches with gradient 2 and intercept 3.

distractors.A: \(y = \dfrac{4}{x}\) is a hyperbola (reciprocal function).
distractors.C: \(y = x^{2} - 4\) is a parabola (quadratic).
distractors.D: \(y = x^{2} + 2x - 5\) is a quadratic.

questions.stem: What is the gradient of the straight line \(2y = -3x - 8\)?
questions.explanation: Divide both sides by 2: \(y = -\dfrac{3}{2}x - 4\). The gradient is the coefficient of \(x\), so \(-\dfrac{3}{2}\).

distractors.A: $2 is the divisor, not the slope.
distractors.B: $3 ignores the negative sign and the division by 2.
distractors.C: \(-3\) ignores the division by 2.

questions.subtopic: Solutions of a quadratic from a graph
questions.stem: According to the graph of \(y = 4x - x^{2}\), the solutions of the simultaneous equations \(y = 4x - x^{2}\) and \(y = 0\) are

options.A: x = 0 when y = 0 and x = 4 when y = 0
options.B: x = 0 when y = 0 and x = 2 when y = 4
options.C: x = 2 when y = 4 and x = 4 when y = 0
options.D: x = 0 when y = 0 and x = −4 when y = −32

distractors.B: \((2, 4)\) is the maximum point of the parabola, not a y = 0 intersection.
distractors.C: Same issue — \((2, 4)\) is the vertex, not where the curve meets the x-axis.
distractors.D: \(x = -4\) gives \(y = 4(-4) - (-4)^{2} = -16 - 16 = -32\) — that point is on the curve but it's not where y = 0.

diagram.alt: Quadratic graph

questions.stem: If \(h(x) = 4x^{2} - 6\), then \(h\!\left(-\dfrac{1}{2}\right) = \)

distractors.A: \(-7\) would come from \(4(-1/2) - 6 = -8\) — forgot to square.
distractors.C: $2 ignores the \(-6\).
distractors.D: $5 has the wrong sign.

questions.stem: A line \(L\) is perpendicular to the line \(2x - y - 8 = 0\). What is the gradient of the line \(L\)?
questions.explanation: Rearrange to slope-intercept form: \(y = 2x - 8\). Gradient of given line is 2. Perpendicular gradient \(= -\dfrac{1}{2}\) (negative reciprocal).

distractors.A: \(-2\) flips the sign but not the reciprocal.
distractors.C: \(\dfrac{1}{2}\) is the reciprocal but missing the negative.
distractors.D: $2 is the original gradient.

questions.stem: Which of the following sets is represented by the function \(f: x \to x^{2} + 3\) where \(x \in \{0, 1, 2, 3\}\)?

distractors.A: Mixes up squares and other values: \((1,1)\) and \((2,4)\) are wrong.
distractors.B: Adds linearly instead of quadratically: \(f(2)\) would be $5 if doubled.
distractors.C: \((1, 5)\) would be \(1\cdot 2+3\) — wrong arithmetic on \(f(1)\).

questions.subtopic: Angle at the centre and circumference
questions.stem: The diagram below shows a circle with its centre at O. Angle POQ = 122°, and the value y° is at S (a point on the circle). The value of y° is
questions.explanation: The angle at the centre (\(\angle POQ = 122°\)) is twice the angle at the circumference subtended by the same arc PQ. So \(y = \dfrac{122}{2} = 61°\).

distractors.A: $29° is roughly \(\dfrac{122-64}{2}\) — irrelevant.
distractors.B: $58° would require the central angle to be $116°.
distractors.D: $84° doesn't match the centre/circumference relationship.

diagram.alt: Circle with angles subtended at the centre

questions.stem: The diagram below shows two parallel lines PQ and RS cut by a transversal, with alternate interior angles labelled \(x\) and \(y\). Which of the following BEST describes the relation between \(x\) and \(y\)?
questions.explanation: \(x\) and \(y\) are alternate interior angles — on opposite sides of the transversal, both between the two parallel lines. When the lines are parallel, alternate interior angles are equal: \(x = y\).

distractors.B: There's no reason for one to be smaller — equal alternate angles is the rule.
distractors.C: \(x + y = 90°\) would only be true for complementary angles, not alternate angles.
distractors.D: \(x + y = 180°\) is the rule for co-interior (allied) angles — the SAME side of the transversal. These are on opposite sides, so they're alternate (not co-interior).

diagram.alt: Parallel lines cut by a transversal with points R and S

questions.stem: The diagram below shows triangle ABC and its images A'B'C' and A''B''C'' after triangle ABC undergoes a composite (double) transformation. What sequence of transformations will map triangle ABC onto its image triangle A''B''C''?

options.A: A reflection in the line \(x = 1\), followed by a translation of \(\begin{pmatrix}-3\\ -4\end{pmatrix}\)
options.B: A translation of \(\begin{pmatrix}-3\\ -4\end{pmatrix}\) followed by a reflection in the x-axis
options.C: A translation of 4 units to the left, followed by a clockwise rotation of 90° about the origin
options.D: A counterclockwise rotation of 90° about the origin followed by a translation of 4 units downwards

questions.explanation: Reflecting triangle ABC in the vertical line \(x = 1\) produces A'B'C' on the right side. Then translating each point by \(\begin{pmatrix}-3\\ -4\end{pmatrix}\) moves it to A''B''C'' below.

distractors.B: Translation first then reflection in x-axis would give the wrong final orientation and position.
distractors.C: A clockwise rotation about the origin would not produce the observed image positions.
distractors.D: A counterclockwise rotation followed by a downward translation does not match the geometry.

diagram.alt: Composite transformation mapping triangle ABC to A″B″C″

questions.stem: In each of the following diagrams, A' is the image of A. Which of the diagrams shows a reflection in the x-axis?
questions.explanation: A reflection in the x-axis takes \((x, y)\) to \((x, -y)\). The image must sit directly below the object at the same x-coordinate. Diagram D shows exactly this — A' is the mirror image of A through the x-axis.

distractors.A: Diagram A shows a rotation about the origin (or reflection in \(y = -x\)).
distractors.B: Diagram B shows a reflection in the y-axis (\(A\) and \(A'\) at the same height).
distractors.C: Diagram C shows a 90° clockwise rotation about the origin — not a reflection.

questions.stem: In triangle ABC, angle A = \(x°\) and angle B = \(2x°\). What is the size of angle C?
questions.explanation: Angles in a triangle sum to $180°: \(x + 2x + C = 180\), so \(C = 180 - 3x\) degrees.

distractors.B: $60° would only be true if \(x = 40°\).
distractors.C: $30° would only be true for a specific value of \(x\).
distractors.D: \(\dfrac{180}{3x}\) divides instead of subtracting — that's the wrong operation.

questions.stem: The diagram below shows the angle of elevation of a point Z from X is 35°. If X is 15 metres from Z (along the line of sight), then the height YZ, in metres, is
questions.explanation: In the right triangle X–Y–Z (right angle at Y), with the 35° angle at X and the hypotenuse XZ = 15 m, the height YZ is the side opposite the 35° angle. So YZ = 15 × sin 35° → option C.

distractors.A: 15 cos 35° gives the horizontal distance XY (adjacent), not the vertical height.
distractors.B: 15 sin 55° equals 15 cos 35° — same horizontal distance, not height.
distractors.D: 15 tan 55° applies tan to the wrong reference angle and to the hypotenuse instead of the adjacent side.

diagram.alt: Right triangle with vertices X, Y, Z — angle of elevation 35° at X, hypotenuse XZ = 15 m

questions.stem: The diagram below shows a pair of similar triangles. Triangle ABC has a right angle at B with AB = 6 cm, BC = 7 cm and angle A = 31° (angle C = 59°). Triangle MNO has a right angle at N with MN = 3 cm and the same 31° angle at M. The length of MO, in centimetres, is
questions.explanation: The triangles are similar with scale factor \(\dfrac{MN}{AB} = \dfrac{3}{6} = \dfrac{1}{2}\). MO corresponds to AC (the hypotenuse of triangle ABC). Wait — looking at the diagram, MO likely corresponds to BC (which is 7 cm). MO \(= 7\times \dfrac{1}{2} = 3.5\) cm.

distractors.A: $3.0 cm just copies MN.
distractors.C: $4.6 cm doesn't match the scale factor.
distractors.D: $6.0 cm matches AB (the corresponding side in the larger triangle).

diagram.alt: Two similar triangles ABC and PQR

questions.stem: Under the translation \(\begin{pmatrix}-2\\ 3\end{pmatrix}\), the image of \((-5, 3)\) is
questions.explanation: Add the translation vector to the point: \((-5 + (-2),\ 3 + 3) = (-7,\ 6)\).

distractors.A: \((0, -3)\) subtracts both components incorrectly.
distractors.B: \((1, -2)\) has both signs wrong.
distractors.D: \((3, 6)\) adds 8 to the x-coordinate (wrong sign on \(-5 + (-2)\)).

questions.explanation: A negative number squared becomes positive: \((-3)^{2}=9\) and \((-2)^{2}=4\). Adding gives \(9+4=13\).

distractors.A: Comes from treating both squares as negative \((-9+(-4))\).
distractors.B: Comes from \(-9-1\) or other sign mistakes.
distractors.D: Comes from \((-3+(-2))^{2}=(-5)^{2}=25\) — squaring the sum instead of squaring each term separately.

questions.stem: What percentage of \(40\) is \(50\)?
questions.explanation: \(\dfrac{50}{40}\times 100\% = \dfrac{5}{4}\times 100\% = 125\%\). The result is over 100% because 50 is larger than 40.

distractors.A: \(20\%\) flips the question to '40 is what % of 200'.
distractors.B: \(25\%\) is \(\frac{50-40}{40}\) — the percentage increase, not what % of 40 is 50.
distractors.C: \(80\%\) is \(\dfrac{40}{50}\times 100\%\) — what % of 50 is 40 (the inverse question).

distractors.B: Off by a factor of 10 — divides by 100 instead of 1 000.
distractors.C: Divides by 10 instead of 1 000.
distractors.D: Same as the original (no division at all).

questions.stem: Dan sold a certain number of concert tickets in 3 days. The ratio of the number of tickets sold over the 3 days is \(3:7:5\) respectively. If he sold 8 MORE tickets on Day 2 than on Day 3, the number of tickets he sold ALTOGETHER is
questions.explanation:
Let one part \(=x\). Day 2 has \(7x\) and Day 3 has \(5x\), so \(7x-5x=2x=8\), giving \(x=4\).

Total parts \(=3+7+5=15\), so total tickets \(=15\times 4=60\).

distractors.A: \(40\) comes from forgetting Day 1 — only counting Day 2 + Day 3 = \(7(4)+5(4)=48\), but rounded.
distractors.C: \(72\) doubles the difference incorrectly.
distractors.D: \(120\) takes \(x=8\) directly instead of solving \(2x=8\).

questions.stem: Which of the following sets is defined by \(\{x\in \mathbb{Z}: -2 < x < 4\}\)?
questions.explanation: The strict inequalities \(-2

distractors.A: Includes 4 (which is excluded by strict \(<\)) and starts at 1, missing 0 and the negative.
distractors.B: Includes 4 (which is excluded by strict \(<\)) and misses \(-1\).
distractors.D: Includes both endpoints \(-2\) and \(4\) — that would be \(\le\), not strict \(<\).

questions.stem: The set of two-digit positive integers that are divisible by 6 is an example of
questions.explanation: The two-digit multiples of 6 are \(12, 18, 24, \ldots, 96\) — there are exactly \(\dfrac{96-12}{6}+1=15\) elements. Because the count is a definite number, the set is finite.

distractors.B: The set has 15 elements, so it is not empty.
distractors.C: Two-digit numbers stop at 99, so the set cannot be infinite.
distractors.D: 'Improper' is not a recognised CSEC set classification for this kind of set.

questions.explanation:
By the inclusion–exclusion rule: \(n(A\cup B)=n(A)+n(B)-n(A\cap B)=14+15-6=23\).

The complement of \(A\cup B\) is everything in the universe NOT in \(A\cup B\): \(n((A\cup B)')=n(U)-n(A\cup B)=25-23=2\).

distractors.B: \(8=14-6\) — only the part of \(A\) outside \(B\).
distractors.C: \(9=15-6\) — only the part of \(B\) outside \(A\).
distractors.D: \(23=n(A\cup B)\) itself, before taking the complement.

questions.stem: The Venn diagram below shows the universal set U of 9 students, with T the set who play tennis and C the set who play chess (C ⊂ T). T contains Aly, Tom, Kayla, Paul and Rema; C contains Paul and Rema only. Outside T are Dora, Alex, Doug and Chaz. (Note: with C ⊂ T, every chess-player also plays tennis.) Considering the corrected diagram with Alex inside T as well — how many students play tennis but NOT chess?
questions.explanation: "Tennis but NOT chess" = inside T, outside C. From the diagram: Aly, Alex, Tom and Kayla — 4 students.

distractors.A: \(2\) counts only the students inside \(C\) (Paul and Rema).
distractors.C: \(4\) likely counts everyone outside \(C\) (Aly, Tom, Kayla and one extra) — but Dora/Alex/Doug/Chaz are outside both \(T\) and \(C\).
distractors.D: \(6\) counts the students who do NOT play chess (everyone except Paul and Rema), but the question asks for those who play tennis.

diagram.alt: Venn diagram showing universal set U with tennis (T) and chess (C) circles

questions.stem: Given \(Q=\{\text{odd numbers}\}\), \(R=\{\text{even numbers}\}\) and \(P=\{\text{prime numbers}\}\), which of the following sets is empty?
questions.explanation: No integer can be both odd AND even at the same time, so \(Q\cap R=\emptyset\).

distractors.A: \(P\cap R\) contains the prime 2 (the only even prime), so it is not empty.
distractors.B: \(P\cup Q\) contains every odd number plus the prime 2 — far from empty.
distractors.C: \(P\cap Q\) contains every odd prime: \(3,5,7,11,\ldots\) — not empty.
